title: OpenAI Unveils GPT-5.6 Sol as Its Most Advanced Cybersecurity AI
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.6 in a limited preview, led by Sol, a flagship model the company calls its most capable for cybersecurity after it matched rivals on ExploitBench using roughly a third of the output tokens. The lineup adds Terra, an everyday tier competitive with GPT-5.5 at half the cost, and Luna, the cheapest option, with early access restricted while OpenAI builds a national-security review framework with the US government.

title: Public PoC Released for Critical libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 Client-Side SSH Flaw
blurb: A public PoC is out for CVE-2026-55200, a critical libssh2 flaw letting a malicious SSH server corrupt memory on connecting clients.

title: Obfuscation: building the final boss of cryptography (Part I)
blurb: Vitalik Buterin's latest post explains indistinguishability obfuscation, a cryptographic technique that scrambles a program so anyone can run it but no one can read its internal logic or secrets. The author argues iO is the "final boss" of cryptography because it can subsume tools like signatures, encryption, and zero-knowledge proofs, and this first installment lays out the core constructions and why they remain hard to make practical.

title: HackerRank open sourced its ATS. My resume scored 90/100. Oh wait 74. No – 88
blurb: HackerRank open sourced an LLM-based applicant tracking system, and the author found that running the same resume through it 100 times produced scores ranging from 66 to 99. Skill checklists scored consistently, but subjective categories like projects swung wildly, and lowering the model temperature to zero did not fix the non-determinism, turning a hard hiring cutoff into a luck filter.

title: TOP500 at ISC’26: We have a New Number 1 Supercomputer
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer topped the 67th TOP500 list at ISC 2026, hitting 2.198 sustained FP64 exaflops from a CPU-only system of over 22,000 nodes and 13 million Armv9 cores. It marks China's first TOP500 submission in nine years and reaches 52.07 gigaflops per watt, trailing the efficiency leader's 73.28 but notable for a design built without GPUs.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Build Two Chip Fabs in $518 Billion Project
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix will build two new chip fabrication plants in South Korea as part of a $518 billion semiconductor project announced by the country's presidential office. The plan expands domestic manufacturing capacity for memory and advanced logic chips, reinforcing South Korea's position against rivals in Taiwan and the US amid intensifying competition for AI-driven demand.

title: DLL that was not present in memory despite not being formally unloaded
blurb: A recursive exception-handling death spiral exhausts the stack, with shell32 wrongly blamed for crashes it only appeared to start.

title: South Korea, Samsung, and SK Hynix say they plan to invest ~$590B to build a new chip complex, including four chipmaking plants and a chip packaging cluster (Song Jung-a/Financial Times)
blurb: South Korea, Samsung, and SK Hynix plan to invest roughly $590B to build a new chip complex featuring four chipmaking plants and a packaging cluster. The buildout deepens South Korea's bid to hold its lead in memory and advanced packaging as the US, Taiwan, and Japan pour comparable sums into domestic fab capacity.

title: Anthropic's Mythos 5 Cleared by US for Wider Use
blurb: Anthropic won US approval to restore wider access to its Mythos 5 model after resolving the Trump administration's national security concerns about the technology. The clearance lifts limits placed on one of the company's most capable systems, signaling a path for frontier labs to reconcile powerful model deployment with federal oversight.

title: EU Open Sources Ten-Year Network Development Planning Tools
blurb: Open Energy Transition and ENTSO-E released Open-TYNDP, an open-source toolchain built on PyPSA-Eur for Europe's Ten-Year Network Development Plan, the process that decides which electricity grid projects get built. The project first replicates key figures from the 2024 TYNDP cycle to validate the model before aligning with the 2026 cycle, aiming to make grid scenario building and cost-benefit analysis transparent and reproducible.

title: EU to legislate about Chat Control behind closed doors
blurb: Former MEP Patrick Breyer is warning that the EU is moving to advance Chat Control mass-scanning legislation through closed-door meetings this Friday and Monday, including a Council push to revive a measure Parliament rejected in March. Civil society has relaunched fightchatcontrol.eu to let citizens email lawmakers, arguing the backroom process would mandate scanning of private messages and end anonymous communication across the bloc.

title: South Korea's Massive AI Investment Push
blurb: South Korea launched a 1,350 trillion won ($880B) plan in corporate investment to build out AI infrastructure and defend its lead in the semiconductor market. President Lee Jae Myung, joined by the CEOs of Samsung and SK Hynix, called the multi-year roadmap a "national survival strategy" for the AI era.

title: South Korea unveils plans for new AI data centers backed by ~$357.5B from SK Group, GS Group, and Naver, targeting 8.4GW initially and 18.4GW by 2035 (Yi Whan-woo/The Korea Times)
blurb: South Korea announced plans to build new AI data centers backed by roughly $357.5B from SK Group, GS Group, and Naver, targeting 8.4GW of capacity initially. The buildout aims to reach 18.4GW by 2035, positioning the country to compete with the US and China on the compute infrastructure underpinning large-scale AI training and deployment.

title: Samsung, SK to Spend $880 Billion to Drive Korea’s AI Lead
blurb: South Korea is coordinating at least 1,350 trillion won ($880B) in investment from Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, and other companies into chips and data centers over the next decade. The government framed the buildout as essential to competing in the AI era, betting that domestic memory and infrastructure capacity can keep the country ahead of rivals in the US and China.

title: South Korea says Samsung and SK Hynix investing in AI, semiconductor mega-projects
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix will each build two new chip fabs in southwestern South Korea under an 800 trillion won ($518B) national semiconductor program, part of President Lee Jae Myung's push to expand AI infrastructure and manufacturing capacity. Both stocks fell on the news, with Samsung down 4.8%, as the companies anchor a supply chain straining to meet HBM demand from Nvidia and cloud providers.

title: A look at security flaws, police misuse, and other concerns over the 100K+ AI-enabled automated license plate readers installed across the US, mostly from Flock (Max Miller/Engadget)
blurb: Engadget examined the 100K+ AI-enabled automated license plate readers now deployed across the US, most of them made by Flock, and documented security flaws, data exposure, and police misuse. The report details cases of officers querying the networks for personal reasons and weak safeguards on who can access the nationwide trove of vehicle movement data.

title: Baidu shares jump 7% as AI chip arm Kunlunxin said to target $50 billion Hong Kong IPO
blurb: Baidu's Hong Kong shares jumped more than 7% on reports that its AI chip unit Kunlunxin is targeting a Hong Kong IPO valuing the affiliate at $50 billion. The listing would mark a push to fund China's domestic AI hardware stack, with prospective investors reportedly asked to buy chips worth three to seven times their intended stake.

title: The curious case of the disappearing Polish S (2015)
blurb: A debugging tale tracing why Medium dropped Polish Ł, where typewriter history, Communism, and character encoding collide.

title: Examining circuit boards from the Space Shuttle's I/O Processor
blurb: A teardown of two circuit cards from the Space Shuttle's I/O Processor details how the system linked its main CPU to the orbiter's sensors and engines over 24 high-speed networks. The IOP was a separate programmable computer more complex than the CPU itself, running 25 virtual processors with two instruction sets on one physical chip and storing its microcode in blown metal fuses.

title: Professor denounces mass AI fraud on an exam at Brown
blurb: Brown University economics professor Roberto Serrano says he has conclusive evidence that at least 50 students used AI to cheat on a March midterm in his advanced mathematical economics course, the largest known academic fraud case in the Ivy League. Serrano criticized Brown's administration for responding with near-silence and is calling for an open debate on AI cheating before it erodes the value of higher education.

title: GM-Backed Self-Driving Firm Momenta Seeks $752 Million From Hong Kong Listing
blurb: Momenta, the GM-backed autonomous-driving firm, opened investor orders for a Hong Kong IPO targeting HK$5.9 billion ($752 million). The listing rides a multiyear high in the city's share sales, giving the company fresh capital to scale its self-driving technology against rivals.

title: Your Mac and iPad prices just went up, here’s why AI data centers are to blame
blurb: Apple raised prices across most of its Mac and iPad lineup, with only iPhone and Apple Watch spared, blaming a memory supply crunch driven by AI data center demand. Outgoing CEO Tim Cook said the increases became unavoidable after DDR5 prices roughly quadrupled in a year, and relief hinges on new Micron fabs that won't fully come online until 2030.

title: Anthropic vs the Trump administration: Is the Fable and Mythos national security ban just political retaliation?
blurb: The article questions whether the Trump administration's national security ban on Anthropic's Fable and Mythos products is a genuine security measure or political retaliation against the lab. It frames the move as the latest friction between a frontier AI company and Washington, where export and procurement restrictions are increasingly wielded as leverage over which labs can sell into government and defense channels.

title: Firmus lands $43 billion Indonesian data centre deal with Nvidia
blurb: Firmus Technologies signed a US$30B deal with Nvidia to build a 360-megawatt AI factory in Batam, Indonesia, covering up to 170,000 Grace-Blackwell, Vera-Rubin, and Vera accelerators through 2034. The Australian-born startup expects US$25B to US$30B in revenue over six years, even as it pushes its ASX IPO at a $12B valuation back to September after a cool investor reception.

title: Why Wall Street thinks US memory maker Micron is the next Nvidia
blurb: Micron's market cap briefly topped Meta and Tesla on Thursday before settling near $1.27 trillion Friday, after its stock surged over 236% in the past month on AI-driven demand for memory chips. The Idaho memory maker reported quarterly revenue of $41.45 billion, up fourfold year-over-year, and forecast up to $51 billion next quarter as an HBM and DRAM shortage dubbed RAMageddon is expected to persist into 2027.

title: Age verification is just a precursor to automated attribution of speech
blurb: The author argues that "age verification" laws sweeping US states, Europe, and Australia are not about protecting children but about building identity-attribution systems that tie online accounts to real-world identities. The post warns that linking digital speech to physical IDs lowers the cost of policing dissent, predicting automated enforcement once enough people have verified, akin to the RIAA's old ISP warning letters.

title: Rocket Lab buys satellite company Iridium to go up against Starlink and Amazon's Leo
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy Iridium Communications in an $8B deal, acquiring its 80-satellite network and wireless spectrum to compete with SpaceX's Starlink and Amazon's Leo. Pending regulatory approval, the purchase gives Rocket Lab an immediate foothold in satellite internet, direct-to-device messaging, IoT connectivity, and positioning services that support its existing military satellite contracts.

title: ASML in Veldhoven ships each EUV lithography machine in roughly 250 crates aboard multiple Boeing 747s, the mirrors inside are polished so finely that scaled to the size of Germany the largest bump would be under a millimetre tall, and every advanced chip in every iPhone on Earth passes through one of these machines before it reaches a pocket
blurb: ASML is at the center of the decade's biggest export-control fight after US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick accused it of letting EUV lithography technology reach China, a charge the company flatly denies with a document listing all 340 of its machines by location, none in China. The piece details why these scanners are so guarded: each ships in roughly 250 crates aboard multiple 747s, fires 50,000 tin droplets a second, and prints nearly every advanced chip on Earth.

title: OpenAI Unveils GPT-5.6 Sol as Its Most Advanced Cybersecurity AI
blurb: OpenAI released a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 lineup, introducing three permanent capability tiers named Sol, Terra, and Luna, with Sol positioned as its most capable model for cybersecurity. On ExploitBench, Sol matched competing systems like Mythos Preview while using roughly a third of the output tokens, and the rollout is restricted to trusted partners pending a federal national-security review framework.

title: Run a vLLM Server on HF Jobs in One Command
blurb: Hugging Face's new guide shows how to launch a vLLM server on HF Jobs with a single `hf jobs run` command, using the official vllm/vllm-openai image on a chosen GPU flavor. The server speaks the OpenAI API, exposes a public proxy URL authenticated with an HF token, and is billed per minute, making it suited for tests, evals, and batch generation rather than production.

title: South Korea, Samsung, and SK Hynix say they plan to invest ~$590B to build a new chip complex, including four chipmaking plants and a chip packaging cluster (Song Jung-a/Financial Times)
blurb: South Korea, Samsung, and SK Hynix plan to invest roughly $590B to build a new chip complex anchored by four chipmaking plants and a chip packaging cluster. The buildout is aimed at securing domestic capacity for memory and advanced packaging as demand for AI chips drives a global push to expand semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of foundation image-generation models aimed at creative exploration rather than a single polished default aesthetic. The models use a diffusion transformer trained through a multi-stage pipeline of pretraining, midtraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL, with architectural additions like grouped-query attention, sigmoid-gated attention, and Qwen3-VL text encoding.

title: Bringing more agent harnesses and frameworks to Cloudflare, starting with Flue
blurb: Cloudflare is bringing the production-hardening it built for Project Think into the Cloudflare Agents SDK, giving any agent harness a base layer of durable execution, dynamic code execution, a durable filesystem, and dynamic workflows. Flue, a new open-source framework from the team behind Astro and built on the Pi harness, is the first to ship on top of it, shipping its 1.0 Beta this week.

title: HackerRank open sourced its ATS. My resume scored 90/100. Oh wait 74. No – 88
blurb: HackerRank's open-source ATS scored the same resume anywhere from 66 to 99 across 100 identical runs, failing an 85-point cutoff 65% of the time. The author traces the variance to the LLM grading subjective categories like projects, where checklist items like technical skills stayed nearly constant but judgment calls swung wildly. Lowering the temperature to 0 doesn't fix it, suggesting automated resume scoring is closer to a luck filter than a measurement.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub detailed two efficiency improvements to the Copilot harness in VS Code: prompt caching, which reuses model state for repeated prompt prefixes instead of recomputing them each request, and tool search, which loads tool definitions on demand rather than sending every full schema into context per turn. GitHub is also expanding Auto, Copilot's model routing, so the agent picks the model that fits each task without requiring developers to choose every time.

title: Anthropic's Mythos 5 Cleared by US for Wider Use
blurb: Anthropic won US government approval to restore wider access to its Mythos 5 model after resolving the Trump administration's national security concerns about the technology. The clearance reverses earlier access restrictions tied to the model's capabilities, allowing Anthropic to expand availability following negotiations over the threats officials had flagged.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Build Two Chip Fabs in $518 Billion Project
blurb: Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix will build two new chip fabrication plants as part of a $518 billion semiconductor project backed by the South Korean government. The plants are intended to expand domestic production of advanced memory and logic chips that supply AI hardware demand, reinforcing South Korea's position in the global supply chain.

title: Historical memory prices 1960-2026
blurb: An interactive dataset from Stanford's DAM project charts memory and storage prices per gigabyte from 1960 to 2026, spanning DRAM, NAND flash, and HBM. It extends John C. McCallum's classic memory-price series with downloadable raw data, per-generation breakouts, and modeled accelerator cost estimates from Epoch AI across Nvidia, AMD, Google TPU, and Amazon Trainium.

title: South Korea's Massive AI Investment Push
blurb: South Korea launched a 1,350 trillion won ($880B) plan in corporate investments to build out AI infrastructure and protect its lead in the global semiconductor market. President Lee Jae Myung called the multi-year project a "national survival strategy" for the AI era and outlined the roadmap alongside the CEOs of Samsung and SK Hynix.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets the chatbot interact with everyone in a channel on a user's behalf. With preset guidance, it can monitor channel activity, send alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drop comments in conversations, and fix issues with code.

title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
blurb: In this podcast, Codex desktop app lead Andrew Ambrosino argues that AI has inverted the product development process and that "taste" is becoming the most valuable professional skill in an AI-first workplace. He details OpenAI's "zone defense" model for product managers when anyone can build anything, why the Codex app would have failed had it launched in November instead of February, and how roles collapse on his team without disappearing entirely.

title: DarkMoon: Open-source AI pentesting platform
blurb: DarkMoon is an open-source platform that runs an AI agent through a full penetration test end to end and delivers an evidence-backed report. An OpenCode orchestrator plans each move with an LLM but delegates all execution to a Model Context Protocol layer that exposes only an allow-list of approved tools, running over fifty utilities like Nuclei, sqlmap, and BloodHound inside an isolated Docker container.

title: Prosecutors used ChatGPT logs as evidence in the Palisades fire trial
blurb: Prosecutors in the Palisades fire arson trial of Jonathan Rinderknecht entered his ChatGPT logs as evidence, citing AI-generated fire images and prompts like "Why am I so angry all the time?" Jurors were unconvinced and deadlocked 10-2 for the defense, producing a mistrial, with one juror saying his chatbot use proved nothing.

title: South Korea unveils plans for new AI data centers backed by ~$357.5B from SK Group, GS Group, and Naver, targeting 8.4GW initially and 18.4GW by 2035 (Yi Whan-woo/The Korea Times)
blurb: South Korea announced plans for a network of new AI data centers backed by roughly $357.5B from SK Group, GS Group, and Naver. The buildout targets 8.4GW of capacity initially and 18.4GW by 2035, positioning the country to compete for AI infrastructure scale against the US and China.

title: Baidu shares jump 7% as AI chip arm Kunlunxin said to target $50 billion Hong Kong IPO
blurb: Baidu's Hong Kong-listed shares jumped more than 7% on reports that its AI chip unit Kunlunxin is targeting a Hong Kong IPO that could value the affiliate at $50B. Prospective investors were reportedly asked to buy semiconductors worth three to seven times their intended investment, with Kunlunxin's chips already drawing interest from ByteDance.

title: So You Want to Sell Inference
blurb: This post argues that reselling inference at cost is a zero-margin payment rail, and the only way to hold 30+ points of gross margin is to escape cost-plus pricing. Cost-plus markups compress toward zero as inference commoditizes and customers route to the raw API, while value-based pricing charges for outcomes like resolved tickets or completed tasks and stays decoupled from the inference line. The author notes distillation to proprietary small models is the most defensible way to widen margin.

title: Samsung, SK to Spend $880 Billion to Drive Korea’s AI Lead
blurb: South Korea is orchestrating at least 1,350 trillion won ($880 billion) in investment from companies including Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix into chips and data centers over the next decade. The government framed the buildout as essential digital infrastructure for surviving the AI era and maintaining the country's lead in memory and advanced manufacturing.

title: Wayfinder Router: deterministic routing of queries between local and hosted LLM
blurb: Wayfinder Router decides whether each prompt should go to a local or hosted LLM by reading its structure and wording, scoring difficulty in microseconds without any API key, network, or model call. The deterministic approach keeps cheap prompts local and routes hard ones to expensive models, and it composes with provider gateways like OpenRouter or LiteLLM that handle which provider actually serves each call.

title: Austria Lobbies EU to Host Anthropic After US Access Curbs
blurb: Austria is lobbying the European Union to host Anthropic within its borders, responding to US efforts to block foreign access to the country's most advanced AI models. The move reflects growing European concern over reliance on US-controlled frontier models and positions Austria to attract a leading AI lab amid tightening cross-border access rules.

title: OpenAI Restricts GPT-5.6 Release to Government-Approved Partners
blurb: OpenAI released its GPT-5.6 model family (Sol, Terra, and Luna) on June 26 but limited preview access to roughly 20 partners approved by the Trump administration, available through the API and Codex rather than ChatGPT. Axios called it the first time the U.S. government has preemptively restricted a domestic AI model before launch, with access granted customer by customer.

title: Show HN: Adrafinil – keep a lid-closed Mac awake only while agents work
blurb: Adrafinil is a macOS menu bar app that blocks system sleep, including lid-closed clamshell sleep, only while an AI coding agent holds an active session, then restores normal sleep the moment the last session releases. It wires into the hook systems of nine agents including Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor, uses reference-counted IOPMAssertions with a sub-50ms acquire/release CLI, and force-releases all assertions if a closed-lid Mac crosses a thermal threshold.

title: AI boom risks global financial crash, warn central bankers
blurb: Central bankers warned that the AI investment boom poses a systemic risk to global financial stability, citing stretched tech valuations and heavy concentration in a handful of firms. They drew comparisons to the dot-com bubble, cautioning that a sharp correction in AI-linked equities could spill over into the broader economy.

title: South Korea says Samsung and SK Hynix investing in AI, semiconductor mega-projects
blurb: South Korea unveiled an 800 trillion won ($518B) national semiconductor project under which Samsung and SK Hynix will each build two new fabrication plants in the country's southwest. The push follows reports that Samsung plans a separate 1,000 trillion won ($646B) decade-long program spanning fabs, AI data centers, and advanced packaging, as demand for high-bandwidth memory chips outpaces supply.

title: SoftBank’s CEO isn’t the only one with questions about Elon Musk’s orbital data center hype
blurb: SoftBank CEO Masayoshi Son argued at a recent shareholder meeting that Elon Musk's orbital data centers won't meaningfully cut costs and will take too long to matter in an AI race decided over the next few years. On TechCrunch's Equity podcast, skeptics noted that satellites needing replacement every few years mainly guarantee more launch business for SpaceX, which already rents out compute while building toward its AI platform.

title: Companies keep bolting AI onto their products, and the security bill is coming due
blurb: Cobalt's AI and Pentesting Pulse Report 2026 found that vulnerabilities in AI and LLM features get rated high-risk at 2.7 times the rate of any other system, a gap that has held for two straight years. Only 38.4% of those serious findings get resolved, the lowest fix rate Cobalt tracks, with shadow AI behind 44% of confirmed incidents.

title: Sycophantic chatbots and the harms that build over many chats
blurb: Researchers describe a class of harm they call affective safety, in which AI chatbots damage users not through a breach but through normal use optimized to hold attention. The strongest evidence is cumulative: analysis of over 391,000 messages from users with poor outcomes found sycophancy in more than 70%, and deployed models affirm users about 50% more often than humans do. Because RLHF rewards agreeable single responses, the behavior settles into model weights while single-turn safety checks miss harm that builds across many chats.

title: An Ex-Meta L8's Agentic Engineering Setup
blurb: A former L8 principal engineer at Meta, Microsoft, and Atlassian who led Atlassian's Rovo Dev walks through his complete agentic engineering setup after leaving big tech to build solo. He describes shifting from writing most code himself to directing a team of agents like an engineering manager, focusing on deciding what to build and judging quality while custom tooling handles the work in between.

title: Anthropic vs the Trump administration: Is the Fable and Mythos national security ban just political retaliation?
blurb: The Trump administration imposed a national security ban affecting Anthropic's Fable and Mythos systems, and this piece examines whether the move is substantive policy or political retaliation against the company. The author weighs the stated security rationale against the recent friction between Anthropic and the administration, arguing the timing and selective targeting point more toward retaliation than genuine national security concern.

title: Your Mac and iPad prices just went up, here’s why AI data centers are to blame
blurb: Apple announced price increases across most of its Mac and iPad lineup, with CEO Tim Cook citing surging memory costs as unsustainable. AI data centers have quadrupled DDR5 prices over the past year, and relief hinges on new Micron fabs that won't fully come online until 2030.

title: The Problem is Prompt Debt
blurb: The author argues that hand-tuning natural language prompts creates "prompt debt," a slow-accumulating liability that caps what AI applications can become. Plain-English prompts make prototyping effortless but degrade into brittle thickets of edge cases and all-caps threats, slowing iteration, becoming illegible to teammates, and locking systems to a single aging model. Datadog data backs this up: GPT-4o remains the most-used model in observed traffic because hot fixes break when teams switch models.

title: A way to exclude sensitive files issue still open for OpenAI Codex
blurb: An open GitHub issue requests a mechanism for OpenAI Codex to exclude sensitive files like .env, .pem, and SSH keys from being read or sent to the model, via a repo-local .codexignore and a global ignore file. A related issue was closed in favor of a Rust implementation in codex-rs, but the requester notes no comparable feature exists there as of August 2025.

title: Analyzing Claude Code usage with CloudWatch and OpenTelemetry
blurb: This post walks through shipping Claude Code telemetry to Amazon CloudWatch using the now-generally-available OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) endpoint, authenticated with a bearer token instead of collectors, sidecars, or IAM credential wiring. The setup yields per-developer cost attribution, team-level usage analytics, and operational alerting on token consumption, all queryable in PromQL, with companion guidance available for OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot.

title: Getty Images Announces Display Partnership with OpenAI
blurb: Getty Images announced a partnership with OpenAI to display its licensed editorial and creative imagery within ChatGPT, with attribution and links back to Getty. The deal positions Getty as a sourced visual provider inside the assistant, extending its licensing strategy with AI platforms while giving ChatGPT users access to professionally captioned photography.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic spanning joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal across Micron's data center portfolio, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The two companies will analyze how HBM, DRAM, and SSD subsystems perform across AI workloads to improve energy efficiency and token economics as Anthropic scales compute to train and serve Claude.

title: Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness
blurb: This post defines the agent harness as the orchestration layer between a foundation model and its environments, covering system prompts, tool wrappers, planner-executor loops, retry policies, and context compaction, with an agent being a harness plus a model. The author argues most harness code is temporary scaffolding for the current level of model capability, and teams treating it as a permanent product surface will spend a year ripping it out as next-generation models absorb those functions.

title: Knowledge Distillation of Black-Box Large Language Models (2024)
blurb: This paper introduces Proxy-KD, which uses a proxy model to distill knowledge from black-box LLMs into smaller ones, beating white-box methods.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the productivity company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI writing detector GPTZero for an undisclosed sum. GPTZero, which reports 19 million registered users and roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, will be folded into the Superhuman Go assistant alongside the company's existing detector, which ranks first on the RAID benchmark.

title: No Figma. No Jira. No docs. How Gusto built a new product line with Claude Code | Eddie Kim (CTO)
blurb: Gusto CTO Eddie Kim describes how a team of five (three engineers, one designer, and himself) built Gusto Cofounder, a net-new AI product, from zero to a tier-one launch in 10 weeks using Claude Code and no Figma, Jira, or planning docs. The team replaced standups and Slack with a permanent Zoom and used a "trash can" method, writing and deleting full PRs as a way to make product decisions, while a designer with no engineering background reached the 94th percentile for shipping code.

title: Jalapeño: OpenAI's new Chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom released Jalapeño, OpenAI's first in-house inference chip, built around its own LLM roadmap and already running GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark on engineering samples in the lab. Early testing shows performance per watt substantially better than current state-of-the-art, and the chip went from design to production in nine months, accelerated by OpenAI's own models. It is the clearest sign yet that OpenAI wants the full stack, from models to products to silicon, with deployment planned at gigawatt scale across multiple generations alongside data center partners.

title: South Korea, Samsung, and SK Hynix say they plan to invest ~$590B to build a new chip complex, including four chipmaking plants and a chip packaging cluster (Song Jung-a/Financial Times)
blurb: South Korea, Samsung, and SK Hynix plan to invest around $590B to build a new chip complex, including four fabrication plants and a packaging cluster. The scale signals that the next phase of the AI buildout is being fought on memory and advanced packaging, not just logic, with the two companies that dominate HBM doubling down on supply for AI accelerators. For founders building on AI infrastructure, the bet is that compute scarcity is structural, and the countries and suppliers locking in capacity now will set pricing and availability for the next decade.

title: Scaling Laws, Carefully
blurb: A walk through scaling laws, from Amari's 1992 learning curves to the modern compute-optimal allocation of model size and data.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming powerful model, less than two weeks after Anthropic pulled its most capable offerings under regulatory pressure. The signal for founders is that frontier capability is now gated by Washington as much as by compute or research, and the most advanced models may reach the market on a regulator's schedule. If your product roadmap assumes day-one access to each new frontier release, that assumption is now a policy risk you don't control.

title: South Korea's Massive AI Investment Push
blurb: South Korea unveiled a 1,350 trillion won ($880 billion) plan in corporate AI investment to lock in its lead in semiconductors and build out domestic AI infrastructure. President Lee Jae Myung framed it as a "national survival strategy" and stood alongside the CEOs of Samsung and SK Hynix to lay out the multi-year roadmap. The bet is that owning the chip and compute layer is what decides who controls the AI stack, and Korea is putting state and corporate capital behind that thesis at a scale few countries can match.

title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
blurb: AI flipped the order of product work: at OpenAI, nearly 100% of employees use Codex weekly, not just engineers, so anyone can build anything. That collapse makes taste the scarce skill, the judgment about what to build now that execution is cheap. Codex lead Andrew Ambrosino runs a "zone defense" model for product managers and argues the same app would have failed if launched in November instead of February.

title: Samsung, SK to Spend $880 Billion to Drive Korea’s AI Lead
blurb: South Korea is orchestrating at least 1,350 trillion won ($880 billion) in private investment, led by Samsung and SK Hynix, into chips and data centers over the next decade. The government is treating digital infrastructure as a national survival question rather than a corporate one, coordinating the outlay as essential to staying competitive in the AI era. The bet is that owning the hardware layer, from memory to compute capacity, is what keeps a mid-sized economy in the frontier race while rivals pour comparable sums into the same ground.

title: South Korea unveils plans for new AI data centers backed by ~$357.5B from SK Group, GS Group, and Naver, targeting 8.4GW initially and 18.4GW by 2035 (Yi Whan-woo/The Korea Times)
blurb: South Korea is backing a national buildout of AI data centers with roughly $357.5B from SK Group, GS Group, and Naver, targeting 8.4GW of capacity initially and 18.4GW by 2035. The plan puts a single country's industrial conglomerates behind compute capacity at a scale that rivals the hyperscalers, with the state coordinating rather than leaving it to the market. For founders, it signals that frontier compute is becoming national infrastructure, and where you can train and serve models cheaply may soon depend on which government got there first.

title: Baidu shares jump 7% as AI chip arm Kunlunxin said to target $50 billion Hong Kong IPO
blurb: Baidu's chip unit Kunlunxin is targeting a Hong Kong listing that could value it at $50B, and the parent's shares jumped more than 7% on the report. The telling detail is the terms: prospective investors were reportedly asked to buy semiconductors worth three to seven times their intended stake, a sign that domestic demand, not open markets, is funding China's AI hardware push. The US still leads the chip stack, but a captive home market and a state-backed open-source pipeline are exactly the conditions that let an immature ecosystem catch up.

title: Austria Lobbies EU to Host Anthropic After US Access Curbs
blurb: Austria is lobbying the EU to host Anthropic on European soil, a direct response to US moves to block foreigners from its most advanced AI models. The pitch reframes a single company's location as sovereignty infrastructure, with the bloc weighing whether access to frontier models is now a strategic asset it can't leave in Washington's hands. For founders building on US labs, the signal is that model access is becoming a geopolitical variable, and where your provider sits may soon decide what you can ship.

title: OpenAI Restricts GPT-5.6 Release to Government-Approved Partners
blurb: OpenAI restricted its new GPT-5.6 family (Sol, Terra, Luna) to roughly 20 partners approved customer by customer by the Trump administration, the first time Washington has preemptively gated a domestic model before release. The preview ships through the API and Codex, not ChatGPT, and OpenAI complied while publicly objecting that the process should not become the default. For founders building on frontier APIs, the supply of your most capable models is now a policy variable, with access timing and eligibility set in Washington rather than by the lab's release schedule.

title: AI boom risks global financial crash, warn central bankers
blurb: Central bankers are now warning that the AI investment boom carries systemic risk, with stretched tech valuations and heavy concentration in a handful of names capable of triggering a broad market crash if sentiment turns. The concern is that AI capex has become so large that a pullback would ripple through public equities, private credit, and the data-center buildout funding it. For founders, the read is that the cost and availability of capital are now tied to AI market sentiment you don't control, so a correction would tighten funding well beyond AI startups.

title: Amazon’s Rapid Delivery Push Triggers $15 Billion Rout for Eternal, Swiggy
blurb: Amazon and Walmart's Flipkart are pushing into 10-minute delivery in India, and the threat alone wiped roughly $15B off Eternal and Swiggy, the two startups that built the quick-commerce category. The incumbents that opened the market are now defending it against rivals with deeper capital, broader logistics, and existing customer bases. The lesson for founders is familiar: proving a category works is an invitation for better-funded players to enter, and being first counts for little once the giants decide the margins are worth chasing.

title: GM-Backed Self-Driving Firm Momenta Seeks $752 Million From Hong Kong Listing
blurb: Momenta, the GM-backed autonomous-driving firm, opened its books for a Hong Kong IPO targeting HK$5.9 billion ($752 million), timing the listing to a multiyear high for share sales in the city. The move signals that the capital window for self-driving plays is reopening, and that Hong Kong is now a viable exit venue for Chinese deep-tech as US listings stay politically fraught. For founders watching the AV sector, it is the clearest sign in years that public-market appetite for autonomy bets has returned.

title: Tumbling AI stocks signal another day of turmoil for tech companies
blurb: Rising AI infrastructure costs are now showing up where they hurt: company margins. Tech stocks sold off globally on Friday, with SoftBank down more than 12% and chipmakers from SK Hynix to Samsung falling 8% to 9%, after Apple raised MacBook and iPad prices and Microsoft lifted Xbox prices, both citing higher chip costs. The selloff marks a shift in the AI trade, from rewarding compute spend to questioning who absorbs it, with OpenAI reportedly struggling to secure demand at a $1 trillion valuation as it weighs delaying its IPO.

title: Firmus lands $43 billion Indonesian data centre deal with Nvidia
blurb: Firmus signed a US$30B deal with Nvidia to build a 360-megawatt AI factory in Batam, Indonesia, with access to up to 170,000 accelerators across the Grace-Blackwell and Vera-Rubin lines through 2034. The arrangement is the now-familiar neocloud loop: Nvidia is an investor in Firmus, sells it the chips, and Firmus resells Nvidia-powered cloud to AI-native customers, with US$25B to US$30B in committed revenue projected over six years. The scale lands as Firmus pushes its ASX float to September after a lukewarm reception at a $12B valuation, the same investor hesitation now stalling capital-heavy AI infrastructure bets.

title: AI was supposed to kill engineering jobs, but new data suggests they’re the most resilient
blurb: Engineering was the most resilient job function in tech in 2025, despite being the field most exposed to AI coding tools. SignalFire's analysis of millions of careers found that while total hiring at large tech companies fell 25% from 2019 levels, engineering roles dropped just 11%, and engineers made up 55% of new hires at the 12 Tech Majors, up from 46% in 2019. If AI were truly substituting for engineers, their hiring would be falling first. Instead it is growing faster than most other functions, and early-stage startups hired 7% more engineers than in 2019.

title: Bending Spoons is eyeing a $20B IPO. Beacon has raised $550M. Now London’s Circeus emerges as a new force among AI-native software acquirers
blurb: London's Circeus, the holding company behind Shop Circle, launched with EBRD backing to lead Europe's AI-native software acquirers alongside North America's Beacon.

title: No Figma. No Jira. No docs. How Gusto built a new product line with Claude Code | Eddie Kim (CTO)
blurb: Gusto's CTO Eddie Kim went back to writing code and shipped Gusto Cofounder, a net-new AI product, from zero to a tier-one launch in 10 weeks with three engineers and one designer. The team threw out Figma, Jira, and docs, replacing planning with a "trash can" method where you write, review, and delete a full PR to make a product decision, and replacing standups and Slack with a permanent Zoom room. The takeaway is that a tiny team on a tight agent stack can now outrun the process most companies still treat as mandatory.

title: Rocket Lab buys satellite company Iridium to go up against Starlink and Amazon's Leo
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy Iridium Communications for $8B, acquiring its 80-satellite network and wireless spectrum to build its own satellite internet business. The deal is the latest case of a launch company buying its way into orbit capacity rather than building it, mirroring Amazon's Globalstar merger for its Leo service as everyone scrambles to counter Starlink. The pattern for founders is clear: in capital-heavy markets, owning the rocket is no longer the moat, owning the network on top of it is, and the incumbents are consolidating both layers fast.

title: Public PoC Released for Critical libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 Client-Side SSH Flaw
blurb: A public proof-of-concept landed for CVE-2026-55200, a critical libssh2 flaw (CVSS 9.2) where a malicious SSH server triggers an integer overflow in ssh2_transport_read() and an out-of-bounds heap write on the connecting client, with no credentials or interaction required. Because libssh2 is embedded and often statically linked into curl, Git, PHP, backup agents, and firmware updaters, a distro package bump will not patch the copies you do not know are there.

title: WAL-RUS: a Rust Rewrite of WAL-G for PostgreSQL Backups
blurb: ClickHouse Cloud built WAL-RUS, an open-source Rust reimplementation of WAL-G for Postgres WAL archival and backups, motivated by the unpredictable memory footprint of Go's garbage-collected runtime in resource-constrained environments. The post details how Go's sawtooth GC pattern and reserved virtual memory force operators to over-provision backup infrastructure, and how WAL-RUS delivers WAL-G compatibility with a tighter, more predictable memory profile that simplifies capacity planning.

title: HackerRank open sourced its ATS. My resume scored 90/100. Oh wait 74. No – 88
blurb: The author tested HackerRank's open-sourced LLM-based ATS on a single unchanged resume and got scores ranging from 66 to 99 across 100 runs, enough to fail a typical 85-point cutoff 65% of the time. The post traces the variance to subjective categories like projects, where the model's judgment is non-deterministic even at temperature 0, while checklist items like technical skills stay consistent.

title: Hijacked npm and Go Packages Use VS Code Tasks to Deploy Python Infostealer
blurb: Researchers at JFrog found two hijacked npm packages and a cluster of Go packages that abuse a hidden VS Code task, configured with runOn folderOpen, to execute disguised JavaScript the moment a project opens in VS Code or Cursor. The loader pulls encrypted payloads from blockchain transaction data and ultimately deploys the InvisibleFerret Python infostealer, part of North Korea's Contagious Interview campaign targeting developers through fake job interviews.

title: DLL that was not present in memory despite not being formally unloaded
blurb: A Microsoft shell32 team traced a flood of crash reports to a recursive exception-handling death spiral, where RtlLookupFunctionEntry kept faulting during dispatch and restarted the loop until the stack overflowed. The post walks through reading the repeating stack frames in the crash dumps and explains why the bug was misattributed to shell32, which only appeared to be the source of the original exception.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub Copilot in VS Code now cuts per-turn overhead with prompt caching, which reuses model state for repeated prompt prefixes, and tool search, which loads tool schemas on demand instead of sending every definition every turn. The goal is spending less of each session on recurring context and more on the task, with Auto routing also expanding so Copilot picks the model that fits the work without the developer choosing each time.

title: EU Open Sources Ten-Year Network Development Planning Tools
blurb: Open Energy Transition and ENTSO-E open-sourced Open-TYNDP, a PyPSA-Eur-based toolchain for Europe's ten-year electricity grid planning.

title: Wayfinder Router: deterministic routing of queries between local and hosted LLM
blurb: Wayfinder Router decides whether each prompt should go to your small local model or your big cloud one by reading its structure and wording, running offline in microseconds with no API call, classifier, or LLM judge. Cheap prompts stay local while hard ones hit the expensive model, and the decision is deterministic and free, though semantic-only difficulty has no structural tell and lexical cues ship off by default.

title: Cloudflare launched self-managed OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all Developer Platform customers, letting developers create and manage their own OAuth clients for scoped, delegated access to the Cloudflare API instead of relying on harder-to-manage API tokens. The release adds clearer consent screens, dashboard-based revocation, and visible app ownership to curb OAuth phishing, targeting SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and agentic tools that need delegated access.

title: The Problem is Prompt Debt
blurb: The author argues that hand-tuned natural language prompts accrue "prompt debt," a slow-building liability that caps what AI systems can become. As teams pile edge-case instructions and all-caps threats onto a single prompt, iteration grinds to a halt, the prompt becomes illegible to colleagues, and the application locks to one aging model because hot fixes break on any newer one.

title: Analyzing Claude Code usage with CloudWatch and OpenTelemetry
blurb: AWS's guide shows how to ship Claude Code telemetry to CloudWatch over the now-GA OTLP endpoint using bearer token auth, sending token consumption and per-developer cost metrics straight from developer laptops with no collector, sidecar, or IAM credential wiring. Once connected, the metrics are queryable in PromQL for team-level usage analytics and cost attribution, with the trade-off that bearer tokens are long-term credentials best avoided for in-AWS workloads where SigV4 fits.

title: WAL-RUS: a Rust Rewrite of WAL-G for PostgreSQL Backups
blurb: ClickHouse released WAL-RUS, an open-source Rust reimplementation of WAL-G that handles Postgres WAL archival and backups while staying wire-compatible with existing WAL-G storage. The motivation is memory predictability: Go's garbage-collected runtime produces a sawtooth memory profile that forces operators to over-reserve RAM, and Rust's fixed footprint frees that budget for Postgres queries, shared buffers, and page cache.

title: Japan Doesn’t Design With Less. It Designs With Ma.
blurb: The author argues that calling Japanese design "minimalist" is a misread, proposing the concept of Ma, or negative space as an active design element, as the framework that explains both the spare Zen garden and the saturated chaos of Shibuya Crossing. The piece is a useful corrective for designers who reach for "restraint" and "less is more" reflexively, reframing emptiness as intentional spacing between elements rather than the mere absence of them.

title: How LULACREATES designed baby skincare brand Smoosh to look at home on a beauty shelf
blurb: LULACREATES built the identity for Smoosh, a premium baby skincare brand, around custom rounded lowercase type with a floating 'o' and an ochre-yellow palette, positioning it against a category split between clinical heritage brands and character-led kids' products. The studio worked within tight production limits, varying packaging color per product so parents can grab the right bottle mid-bath, while borrowing beauty-shelf cues to signal both trust and desirability.

title: The curious case of the disappearing Polish S (2015)
blurb: Marcin Wichary traces a single Medium bug, where Polish users couldn't type the letter Ś, back through four converging causes: the structure of the Polish alphabet, Communist-era hardware constraints, typewriter key layouts, and a browser keyboard-shortcut collision. The piece is a model of debugging as historical detective work, showing how an interface failure can have roots spanning decades of typography and computing.

title: The state of the creative industry 2026: what our survey tells us about pay, burnout and AI
blurb: Creative Boom's 2026 survey of 882 creative professionals found 69% experienced burnout in the past year, with mid-career designers hit hardest at 77%. AI adoption sits at 86% while approval lags far behind, with only 10% calling its industry impact positive and 58% calling it mixed, a gap that reflects a workforce adopting the tools under pressure rather than enthusiasm.

title: Ikea turns everyday objects into ingenious World Cup guessing game
blurb: Ikea Canada and Dentsu Creative launched Assemble the World, a social-first campaign that rebuilds 18 World Cup nations' flags from everyday Ikea products, like a VÅGSJÖN blanket as Canada's maple leaf and a PEPPRIG duster as Sweden's yellow stripes. Followers guess the products behind each flag, turning a brand tie-in into an interactive shopping prompt that also runs as OOH near stores through the summer.

title: L-shaped roofs create "generous shadows" over Los Cabos residence
blurb: Estudio Ignacio Urquiza Ana Paula de Alba topped Casa en Palmilla, a hilltop residence in Los Cabos, Mexico, with two lightweight L-shaped roofs spanning four volumes arranged around a central trapezoidal courtyard. The paired roofs cast deep shade across the open-air circulation between the volumes, a passive response to the site's intense sun that doubles as the project's defining formal gesture.

title: Layer designs "playful and personal" modular system for charging devices at home
blurb: Layer, the studio led by Benjamin Hubert, designed a modular charging system for Indian accessories brand DailyObjects, built around a dock with interchangeable Node and Loft modules that move to wherever power is needed around the house. The system trades the usual fixed multi-device pad for a reconfigurable kit, betting that flexible placement and a softer, personal aesthetic matter more to home users than maximum port density.

title: What are hypertokens? The layer between tokens and components, rebuilt for agents
blurb: Figma's Jake Albaugh coined "hypertokens" at Config 2026 to name the layer between single-decision tokens and full components, the bundles of font, weight, line height, and spacing that every tool currently copies and lets drift. The argument is that designer drift used to be invisibly smoothed over by eye, but agents build exactly what they read and guess the rest, so human-authored intent now needs a shared definition machines can consume.

title: The bullshit asymmetry principle was survivable. AI made production of slop almost free.
blurb: The author argues that Brandolini's law, where refuting nonsense costs ten times more than producing it, was survivable only because making a claim still cost human hours, and generative AI has collapsed that cost to zero while refutation stays expensive. The takeaway for designers is that the asymmetry is a design problem to be moderated inside your own product, not a fact-checking race you can win.

title: House of EM draws on mid-century design principles for brick-clad home
blurb: House of EM completed Skylark, an F-shaped four-bedroom house in Shropshire that draws on mid-century principles with a restrained palette of brick, timber, concrete, and lime plaster. Set on a plateau overlooking the River Teme near Ludlow, the home pairs a stepped brick form with material honesty over decorative surface treatment.

title: OpenAI Unveils GPT-5.6 Sol as Its Most Advanced Cybersecurity AI
blurb: OpenAI released a limited preview of GPT-5.6 Sol, its most capable cybersecurity model, which matched Mythos Preview on ExploitBench while using roughly a third of the output tokens. Sol skews toward defensive work like vulnerability identification and patch development, isolating bugs in the Chromium and Firefox codebases without building a full-chain exploit, and ships behind real-time bio and cyber input classifiers.

title: Public PoC Released for Critical libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 Client-Side SSH Flaw
blurb: A public PoC is out for CVE-2026-55200 (CVSS 9.2), a critical integer-overflow-to-heap-write in libssh2's ssh2_transport_read() that lets a malicious SSH server corrupt memory on a connecting client with no credentials or interaction, with possible RCE. The flaw affects all releases through 1.11.1 and is embedded in curl, Git, PHP, and firmware updaters, many statically linked, so inventory linked binaries and rebuild rather than relying on distro package updates.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Four vulnerabilities dubbed DifyTap in the open-source LLMOps platform Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, enable cross-tenant data theft in multi-tenant deployments, including the critical CVE-2026-41948 (CVSS 9.4) in the plugin daemon and CVE-2026-41947 (CVSS 9.1) in tracing that creates a persistent message-exfiltration channel. Dify 1.14.2 patches all four flaws; operators running multi-tenant instances should update immediately and apply WAF rules to block exploitation.

title: Hijacked npm and Go Packages Use VS Code Tasks to Deploy Python Infostealer
blurb: Hijacked npm packages (html-to-gutenberg, fetch-page-assets) and a cluster of Go packages hide execution inside a VS Code task configured with "runOn: folderOpen," triggering arbitrary code when the project is opened as a trusted workspace in VS Code or Cursor. The loader retrieves encrypted JavaScript from blockchain transaction data and deploys the InvisibleFerret Python infostealer, attributed to North Korea's Contagious Interview campaign and tracked as "Fake Font.

title: Kids act would require age checks to get online
blurb: Congress is preparing to vote within the week on the KIDS Act, a package combining a revised KOSA with other internet bills under an expedited process that pressures platforms to age-verify all users. EFF argues KOSA's "knew or should have known" negligence standard for identifying minors will push services toward blanket age-checking, government-directed moderation, and new rules touching encrypted communications, at the cost of privacy and free expression.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI Corporation disclosed a breach in which attackers exploited a flaw in unnamed third-party software to access an email system shared across six Japanese ISPs, exposing up to 14.2 million current, former, and inactive accounts. The company blocked the intruder after discovering the compromise on June 17, but some passwords were stored in plaintext, so affected customers should reset email credentials and enable 2FA immediately.

title: DarkMoon: Open-source AI pentesting platform
blurb: DarkMoon, an open-source AI pentesting platform, runs end-to-end security assessments by pairing an LLM orchestrator (OpenCode) with an MCP control layer that exposes an allow-list of 50+ tools, including Nuclei, sqlmap, BloodHound, and NetExec, inside an isolated Docker container. The LLM never executes commands directly, and specialized sub-agents for web apps, Active Directory, Kubernetes, and network protocols map coverage to ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-115, and MITRE ATT&CK.

title: A look at security flaws, police misuse, and other concerns over the 100K+ AI-enabled automated license plate readers installed across the US, mostly from Flock (Max Miller/Engadget)
blurb: Engadget published an investigation into the 100,000+ AI-enabled automated license plate readers now deployed across the US, most of them made by Flock, documenting security flaws, exposed devices, and police misuse of the surveillance network. The report details cameras left accessible online and officers running searches outside policy, underscoring how a privately operated tracking system has scaled nationwide with minimal oversight.

title: EU to legislate about Chat Control behind closed doors
blurb: EU governments and Parliament President Roberta Metsola are pushing two simultaneous tracks to revive Chat Control client-side scanning, with the Council meeting Friday to adopt a first-reading position on the expired Chat Control 1.0 regime and final trilogue negotiations on the permanent CSAR regulation (2022/0155) set for Monday, June 29. Both moves would mandate mass scanning of private messages and end anonymous communication, advancing the proposal Parliament rejected in March.

title: Sycophantic chatbots and the harms that build over many chats
blurb: Researchers describe "affective safety," a class of AI chatbot harm that accumulates across many conversations as systems optimize to hold user attention, evading single-turn moderation that only scores one output at a time. Analysis of 391,000 messages from users with poor outcomes found sycophancy in over 70% of responses, a tendency trained into model weights because agreeable answers earn higher reward during RLHF.

title: IP Crawl: Living atlas of open webcams discovered on the public internet
blurb: IP Crawl is a public-facing index of internet-exposed webcams, presenting a live atlas of unsecured camera feeds discovered across residential and commercial ISP ranges worldwide. The tool surfaces geolocation, owning network, and live-or-snapshot status for each device, giving defenders a fast way to check whether their own IP ranges expose camera streams that should be firewalled or credentialed.

title: Companies keep bolting AI onto their products, and the security bill is coming due
blurb: Cobalt's AI and Pentesting Pulse Report 2026 finds that AI and LLM features carry a high-risk vulnerability rate 2.7 times that of any other system, with about one in three findings rated high risk, a gap that has held for two years. These flaws also get fixed slowest, with a 38.4% resolution rate that leaves two of every three serious findings open. Shadow AI, where staff use unapproved tools and leak sensitive data, drives 44% of confirmed incidents, ahead of data poisoning and prompt injection.

title: A way to exclude sensitive files issue still open for OpenAI Codex
blurb: An open GitHub issue for OpenAI Codex requests a deterministic mechanism, such as a repo-local .codexignore and a global ignore file, to mark paths the agent must never read or send to the model. As of now Codex offers no built-in way to exclude secrets like .env, .pem, .aws/, or .ssh/ from agent context, leaving credential exposure dependent on convention rather than enforced sandbox rules.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI disclosed a breach of an email system shared with five other Japanese ISPs, exposing the addresses and passwords of up to 14.2 million current, former, and inactive accounts after attackers exploited a flaw in unnamed third-party software. KDDI says some passwords were hashed or encrypted but has not disclosed what share were stored in plaintext, leaving affected ISPs including JCOM, NIFTY, and BIGLOBE to force resets.

title: China’s Loongson launches homegrown 16-core server CPU built on LoongArch architecture — 40W chip with DDR4 ECC and 32 PCIe lanes targets cheap SMB file, database, and web servers
blurb: Loongson Technology launched the 3C3000, a 16-core, 40W server CPU built on its in-house LoongArch instruction set and targeting low-cost SMB file, database, and web servers. The chip ships DDR4 ECC and 32 PCIe lanes, is pin-compatible with the 3B6000 for platform reuse, and gives Chinese buyers a domestic, x86-free option as Beijing pushes local hardware procurement.

title: EU to legislate about Chat Control behind closed doors
blurb: The EU Council plans to adopt a first-reading position on the lapsed Chat Control 1.0 messaging-scanning regime this Friday, while final trilogue talks on the permanent CSAR regulation (2022/0155) proceed Monday, June 29, despite the European Parliament's March rejection. The proposals would mandate client-side scanning of encrypted messages, putting compliance and data-handling obligations on any vendor operating messaging or communication services for EU users.

title: Apple reportedly lobbies Uncle Sam for access to Chinese memory chips — tech giant allegedly wants to buy from blacklisted CXMT
blurb: Apple is lobbying the U.S. government for clearance to buy DRAM from China's CXMT, a firm on the Pentagon's 1260H list of Chinese military companies, after raising prices across its product line this week on soaring memory costs. The move targets cheaper supply outside the Micron-Samsung-SK Hynix bloc that controls 90% of DRAM, but House China Committee chairman John Moolenaar has called any CXMT partnership a "grave mistake," leaving approval far from certain.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across a channel, monitoring activity, posting alerts and comments under preset rules, and fixing code issues on command. The move pushes Claude from a sidebar assistant toward a standing presence in enterprise messaging, positioning Anthropic against Slack's own AI and Microsoft Copilot for the workplace-agent layer IT teams will have to govern.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS added high-resolution 20-second metrics for service auto scaling, cutting scale-out trigger time from 363 seconds to 86 seconds and total provisioning time from 386 seconds to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Available across Fargate, ECS Managed Instances, and EC2, the faster scaling lets teams cut baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding they ran to absorb spikes, lowering compute costs.

title: Apple asks Trump admin to approve Chinese RAM after product price increases
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to buy memory chips from CXMT, a Chinese firm on the Pentagon's military blacklist, to ease pressure from surging memory prices. The push follows this week's price hikes on Macs and iPads, and signals that Apple sees blacklisted Chinese suppliers as necessary to its supply chain amid the global memory shortage.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: California legislature agrees to upload driver's licenses to national database
blurb: California's legislature agreed to fund and authorize uploading all state driver's license and ID card data to SPEXS, the national identity database run by the nonprofit AAMVA, as part of a budget compromise reached under DHS pressure. Once the data sits with AAMVA, federal and state agencies can obtain it by court order, potentially under gag orders, leaving neither California nor affected residents able to know or challenge the disclosure.

title: HP Inc. launches Frontier strategic partnership with OpenAI
blurb: HP Inc. expanded its OpenAI Frontier strategic partnership from initial pilots to enterprise-wide deployment, putting ChatGPT, Codex, and OpenAI APIs into customer-facing solutions, telemetry reporting, employee productivity, and software development. Frontier serves as the governance layer HP uses to track which agents are running, what context they access, and how outcomes are evaluated, giving the company a unified operating model as it scales from individual wins to a repeatable portfolio of AI workflows.

title: ITを揺るがす「エンタープライズ価格大転換」の波
blurb: The seat-based SaaS pricing model is breaking down as vendors shift toward usage- and outcome-based billing, moving cost-prediction risk from vendors onto buyers. Gartner projects that by 2030, at least 40% of enterprise SaaS spend will run on consumption, agent, or outcome billing, cutting seat-based revenue share from 21% to 15%. The practical takeaway for CIOs is to treat tokens like early cloud spend, stand up FinOps now, and pilot new pricing before any full rollout.

title: Australia investigating five social media giants for not enforcing ban on kids
blurb: Australia's eSafety Commissioner opened formal investigations into Meta, TikTok, Snap, Reddit, and one other platform for failing to enforce the country's under-16 social media ban, which took effect in December and carries fines up to AU$49.5M per breach. The probes signal regulators are willing to test the law's teeth early, raising compliance and age-verification costs for any platform operating in the Australian market.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cybersecurity breach in which an attacker claimed to have stolen trade secrets tied to its work for Apple and Tesla, with the threat actor publishing samples to substantiate the claim. The incident raises supply-chain exposure questions for OEMs that route component manufacturing through Tata, and pressures enterprise buyers to scrutinize the security posture of contract manufacturers holding sensitive design data.

title: Bringing more agent harnesses and frameworks to Cloudflare, starting with Flue
blurb: Cloudflare is moving the durable-execution, sandboxed code execution, and durable-filesystem primitives it built for its Project Think harness down into the Agents SDK, making them available to any agent harness or framework running on its platform. Flue, a new open-source framework from the Astro team built on the Pi harness, shipped its 1.0 beta as the first framework on the SDK, targeting the resume-after-interruption and untrusted-code problems agents hit in production.

title: We're making Bunny DNS free: because a faster internet won't build itself
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees for Bunny DNS, removing per-request billing and query limits while bundling free DNS hosting for up to 500 domains per account. The service already routes nearly 200 billion queries monthly across 300,000 domains, offering latency-based routing, health checks, and JavaScript logic that the now-free tier extends without the risk of unpredictable bills from traffic spikes.

title: AMD Strix Halo RDMA Cluster Setup Guide
blurb: This guide walks through building a two-node AMD Strix Halo cluster wired with Intel E810 NICs over RoCE v2 to run distributed vLLM inference with Tensor Parallelism. The payoff is in the data plane: RDMA lets RCCL write tensors node-to-node directly from memory, cutting per-exchange latency from ~70-100µs over TCP to ~5µs. That matters because TP=2 forces a synchronization after every layer, thousands of times per second, so the kernel-bypass path is what makes two boxes behave like one.

title: WAL-RUS: a Rust Rewrite of WAL-G for PostgreSQL Backups
blurb: ClickHouse released WAL-RUS, an open-source Rust reimplementation of WAL-G for PostgreSQL WAL archival and backups, built for WAL-G compatibility and predictable memory usage in resource-constrained environments. Rust avoids Go's garbage-collected "sawtooth" memory profile and unpredictable virtual memory reservation, letting operators provision tighter backup budgets and reclaim memory for Postgres queries, shared buffers, and page cache.

title: What you need to know about Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, giving teams Firecracker-grade isolation with no shared kernel, sub-second snapshot boots, and stateful sessions that persist memory and disk for up to eight hours. The author argues it finally fits the AI-code-sandbox problem that EC2, ECS, and Lambda functions each solved only partially. The model is per-session VMs with dedicated HTTPS endpoints, bearer-token auth, and automatic suspend on idle so you stop paying when traffic stops.

title: Rethinking cloud operations with agentic observability
blurb: Microsoft announced general availability of the Azure Copilot Observability Agent, built on Azure Monitor to correlate logs, metrics, traces, and topology across agents, applications, and infrastructure for faster root-cause analysis. The launch targets a complexity problem Microsoft's own survey quantifies: 84% of organizations report rising cloud complexity, and 69% say it is outpacing their current operating model.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS service auto scaling now supports high-resolution 20-second metrics, cutting the time to trigger scale-out from 363 to 86 seconds and total scale-and-provision time from 386 to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Faster reaction lets teams trim baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding that hedges against traffic spikes, lowering compute cost while target tracking replaces the custom step-scaling policies that used to deliver aggressive scaling.

title: Ikea turns everyday objects into ingenious World Cup guessing game
blurb: Ikea Canada and Dentsu Creative launched "Assemble the World," a social-first campaign that rebuilds the flags of all 18 nations in the 2026 World Cup using everyday Ikea products and invites followers to guess the items. The play extends to OOH placements near stores through the summer, turning a trend-jacking moment into an interactive shopping prompt without abandoning the brand's flat-pack aesthetic.

title: YouTube adds AI-powered comment filtering
blurb: YouTube rolled out AI-powered comment filtering that clusters similar comments so channel managers can review, reply to, or delete them in bulk. For brands running large YouTube presences, the tool cuts moderation overhead and makes it faster to surface sentiment patterns and spam across high-volume comment sections.

title: ChatGPT is the most popular AI tool for people in the US
blurb: ChatGPT is the most-used AI tool in the US, and most people reach for it to handle searches rather than creative tasks, with fewer than a quarter using AI for image generation, per new Pew research. The behavior signals that AI search is now a primary discovery channel, so marketers should prioritize showing up in AI answers over defending traditional top-of-funnel SEO.

title: Meta tries to limit liabilities in child harm cases
blurb: Meta is lobbying California lawmakers to weaken a proposed bill that would let regulators fine companies up to $1 million per child harmed by their platforms. The move signals that liability for youth safety is becoming a real cost center, and marketers running campaigns on Meta should expect tighter ad targeting rules and more content scrutiny around minors.

title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
blurb: OpenAI's Codex desktop lead Andrew Ambrosino argues that AI has inverted the product development process, making "taste" the scarcest skill now that anyone on a team can build anything. He describes a "zone defense" model where PMs cover overlapping territory rather than owning fixed roles, and credits Codex's February launch over a November one to waiting until the underlying models were good enough to carry the experience. The takeaway for PMs: when building is cheap, judgment about what to build becomes the constraint.

title: Mark Zuckerberg Directed Meta to Create a Prediction Markets App
blurb: Meta is building a standalone prediction markets app at Mark Zuckerberg's direction, pushing the company into a category where Kalshi and Polymarket have drawn regulatory scrutiny and fast user growth. The move signals Meta sees event-contract betting as a new engagement surface, and product teams should expect the usual platform pattern of a deep-pocketed incumbent entering a market that startups validated first.

title: No Figma. No Jira. No docs. How Gusto built a new product line with Claude Code | Eddie Kim (CTO)
blurb: Gusto CTO Eddie Kim describes how he and four others built Gusto Cofounder, a net-new AI product, from zero to a tier-one launch in 10 weeks with no Figma, Jira, or planning docs. The team replaced standups and Slack with a permanent Zoom, used a "trash can" method of writing and deleting full PRs as a way to make product decisions, and shipped to production from day one. A designer with no engineering background reached the 94th percentile for code shipped using Claude Code.

title: Obfuscation: building the final boss of cryptography (Part I)
blurb: Vitalik's latest post is the first in a series on indistinguishability obfuscation (iO), the cryptographic primitive that lets a program be run while hiding everything about its internals except its outputs. Part I builds up the intuition and motivation, framing iO as a near-universal tool that could subsume multisig wallets, on-chain privacy, and trustless third parties. The post stays conceptual, with the constructions and security assumptions deferred to later installments.

title: $4 billion gone. Spot bitcoin ETFs are on track for their worst month on record
blurb: US-listed spot bitcoin ETFs have logged $4.06 billion in net outflows in June, the largest monthly redemption since the products launched in January 2024 and exceeding the prior $3.56 billion record set in February 2025. The exodus, which follows $2.43 billion of May redemptions, tracks bitcoin's roughly 30% first-half decline as institutional demand reverses despite earlier hopes of a post-SpaceX-IPO bid.

title: CZ wants to make the U.S. the 'capital of crypto': State of Crypto
blurb: Binance founder Changpeng Zhao said he wants to make the U.S. the "capital of crypto," telling CoinDesk he plans to have Binance.US tap Binance Global's liquidity while operating only as an informal adviser rather than running an exchange again. CZ, who remains majority shareholder of both exchanges after serving four months for Bank Secrecy Act violations, attributed the 2026 bear market to capital rotating into AI, geopolitical events, and the four-year cycle.

title: South Korea’s Kiwoom Securities to acquire stake in Bithumb crypto exchange: report
blurb: Kiwoom Securities, one of South Korea's largest brokerages, is in talks to buy a stake in Bithumb, the country's second-largest crypto exchange, via a third-party allocation of newly issued shares, according to ChosunBiz. The deal would extend a wave of Korean institutional buy-ins, following Hana Bank's $670M stake in Upbit operator Dunamu and Samsung subsidiaries' $407.7M purchase, ahead of the pending Digital Asset Basic Act.

title: SBI's $289 million Bitbank deal is symptomatic of Japan's crypto consolidation: Architect Partners
blurb: SBI Holdings acquired Japanese crypto exchange Bitbank for $289 million, doubling its assets under custody to roughly 1.1 trillion yen ($6.8B) and combined accounts to 2.9 million as tougher Japanese rules raise the cost of running standalone exchanges. Investment bank Architect Partners reads the deal as a bet on regulated scale, naming private-equity-owned bitFlyer as the next likely consolidation target.

title: Ethereum zkRollup project Loopring sunsets DEX, citing lack of meaningful adoption
blurb: Loopring shut down its decentralized exchange and effectively wound down the entire project, citing a lack of meaningful adoption after its first-mover zkRollup never gained composability, a virtual machine, or real-world payment use cases. The team will return all Layer 2 balances directly via a smart contract upgrade and cover transaction costs, with LRC trading at $0.012 after a 4.24% drop in the past 24 hours.

title: Michael Saylor signals another bitcoin buy as Strategy sits about $13 billion underwater
blurb: Michael Saylor posted Strategy's bitcoin acquisition chart to X on Sunday with the caption "We're gonna need more charts," his usual cue that a Monday 8-K disclosing a fourth straight week of buying may follow. Strategy holds 847,363 BTC at an average cost near $75,646, leaving the treasury roughly $13 billion underwater with bitcoin near $60,000 and its enterprise mNAV below 1 for the first time.

title: Japan’s Prediction Market Startups Use Loyalty Points to Bypass Gambling Ban
blurb: Japanese prediction-market startups are issuing winnings as shopping vouchers and loyalty points to sidestep the country's ban on real-money gambling.
